journal_entries.text: Acquired a bill of landing at Nexus Prime. Cost 250 credits for the privilege of docking and trading. Officer Larsen warned about unarmed travel through dangerous systems.
Delivered 10 units of iron ore to Forgeworks for a cargo broker at Nexus Prime. 500 credits earned. First completed contract.
Met Elena Reeves at Nexus Prime. Freelance navigator, ex-Commerce Guild. Offered to optimize routes in exchange for a berth on the ship.
Earned trade access at Breakstone by delivering food to the miners' commissary. Hanna Voss runs the docks here. The Union judges people by what they do, not what they say.

Met Marcus Jin at Breakstone. Mining foreman, twenty years on station. He filed an engineering report about the air recyclers on the Nexus Prime orbital. The report was buried. Six months later, my father was dead.

Transported Dr. Priya Osei to Axiom Labs with time-sensitive research samples. She studies resource extraction economics. Describes working conditions as data sets. Axiom Labs is clinical and precise. Nothing like Breakstone.
Met Tomas Drifter at Haven's Rest. Accepted a gray-market trade. Rerouting supplies past Guild tariffs to people who need them. Haven's Rest runs on handshakes and trust.
Met Tomas Drifter at Haven's Rest. Declined his offer to reroute supplies around Guild tariffs. The rules exist for a reason, even if the reasons are not always fair.
Responded to a distress signal from a freighter under pirate attack. Met Captain Reva Sato, Guild military escort. Three convoy attacks this month, same pattern. Someone knows the patrol routes.
Passed a freighter sending a distress signal. Later heard Captain Reva Sato arrived too late. Three convoy attacks this month. The pattern is impossible to ignore.
Dex Halloran, information broker, approached at the Nexus Prime cantina. Offered intel on the pirate attacks in exchange for a delivery to Crimson Reach. No questions asked.
Delivered a package to Malia Torres at Crimson Reach. She says whoever is backing the pirates has serious resources. Real money, real ships, real intelligence. This is not random raiding.
Attended faction summit at Axiom Labs. All four factions present. No agreement reached. Guild wants military response, Union wants solidarity, Collective wants data, Alliance wants independence.
Ambushed by pirates during travel. Survived the attack. Their ships were well-equipped. Better than any frontier raiders should be.
Found Oren Tak in Breakstone's mining tunnels. Retired miner, paranoid but observant. He has seen unmarked ships docking at a hidden facility near Iron Depths. Guild-class hulls.
Located a pirate command base near Iron Depths. Scan data confirms it is an active operations center. Hard evidence.
Presented evidence of the pirate base to faction leaders. Chose an ally for the operation ahead.

The pirate operation is funded by a rogue faction within the Commerce Guild. They call themselves The Ledger. Goal: destabilize shipping, monopolize security, control the Expanse. Base neutralized. Leaders escaped.

Act One concluded. The Ledger is exposed but not destroyed. Uncharted coordinates recovered from the base point to more operations beyond the Expanse's borders. The factions are talking. It is a start.

Sold the cargo broker's iron ore instead of delivering it to Forgeworks. Odom flagged my permit with a freight violation. Word will spread among the brokers. Not the reputation I wanted to build on my first week in the Expanse.

What Arna told me about running hot. Three legality tiers: legal moves anywhere; restricted needs papers (faction permits, customs stamps, full manifest trail); illegal is contraband (weapons, narcotics, biohazards, stolen goods). Penalties escalate: warn, fine, confiscate, ban. Each faction enforces differently. Every system has an inspection rate tied to its enforcement. Heat builds when you run dirty and decays when you do not. Smuggling compartments installed at shipyards hide contraband from routine checks (persistent searches still find it). Black markets are hidden behind rep or introductions. She taught me all of this while bleeding through a compress.

Arna has a younger brother on a Union refinery station. His lungs are giving out from processed air. Union healthcare gave him two options: keep working or pay out of pocket for transfer. The transfer costs more than she makes in ten years. The ore, the refining, the sale, the ambush, all of it was for him. She hid that like she hides everything, because telling would have meant admitting she was desperate.

Went back to Heron's Mark. Keren was waiting, thought it was quiet. It wasn't. The depot held more than the sale was worth and the Crimson Reach will notice. Arna is packing a bag now. Two blacklists instead of one. She said something smaller than I expected and walked off toward the dock.

Clean pull at a derelict in Havens Rest. Nothing followed us out. Arna stayed behind at the manifest desk the whole time. When I got back she called it a fair trade for a scar and a lesson. She is not going to save her brother this way. She said so. She is staying anyway.

I turned Arna in. She did not see it coming. She wouldn't have. She said 'oh, that's what this is' and put her hands flat on the terminal. She didn't look at me. I didn't look at her either. I got paid. The docks kept running.

Said goodbye to Arna at the departure bay. She had a duffel bag at her feet. Not much else. She told me about the pendant before she went: it was her mother's, not any of the three stories she'd told me. She said she wanted one thing she'd said to me to be true. Then she walked toward the freighter bound for Port Kestrel and she did not look back. She never wrote the letter to her brother. He is still waiting.

Naveen Prakash confirmed what the restricted sector's maintenance logs imply: the DCMC reclassification does not match any standard hazard assessment. Someone cleared the records using a document form he has only seen once before, in a review that was sealed weeks after he submitted his findings. He has filed four supplementary notes. None of them have been read.

Yuki Tanaka flagged an anomaly at Nova Research. A deep-field reconnaissance signal was routed directly to the restricted wing, bypassing the shared logging repository. Her discrepancy report was acknowledged, then the acknowledgment's own access log was stripped. Someone in that wing has clearance that does not appear on any org chart she can access.

Turned in my first contract at the Wreckers' Guild Hall. Malia weighed the haul, logged it, paid me out. No ceremony. She said this is what the work looks like. Standing builds slow. Down here, slow counts.

Journeyman now. Malia signed off on it without making a thing of it. Recovery pulls open up. Higher-margin work, higher exposure. She said journeyman is when the Guild starts trusting you with the bigger pieces.

Master tier at the Guild Hall. Malia stopped calling me kid. She did not say so, just stopped. Deep-derelict work is on the board now. Old hulls, bad orientations, partial atmosphere. The kind of pulls that need a name on them.

Squared the missed contract with Malia. She did not lecture. She just opened the board back up and said the Guild forgets a debt once. Then she handed me a torch and asked me to hold the line on the next one.

Sten Brygaard told the story the way he tells it. Cargo Bay 7. Third shift, morning cycle. Sora Takahashi climbed up on a shipping crate. She was short. Everyone forgets how short she was, he said. She told the bay: we built this, all of it, and we are done letting other people decide what it is worth. That was the speech. Sten said it twice, the second time for me to hear it.

Sten said: nineteen days. He did not say it was a strike. He said it was nineteen days of people not coming up out of the shafts. He said: they did it worker by worker. Nobody made a list of heroes. The list would have been the whole roster. The Charter signed because the bays were quiet for nineteen days, not because of one speech.

Sten poured a second cup. He said: the Breakstone Charter, 2267. The signing was in this bay. Sora signed first because she happened to be standing closest to the table. He says she would have hated being the symbol. He says she got over it.

Sten said the collapse came forty years after the Charter. Section 6 went down on a third shift. Fourteen people. We got eleven names on that wall, he said. Three were never recovered. He pointed at the wall. He did not read the names out. He said: every shift change, somebody puts their hand on it. That is the rotation.

Sten said: Marcus Jin's father was at the walkout. Stayed the full nineteen days. So was a thousand other people, he said. Marcus knows what the Union owes him and what it does not. He said the work continues. He said: come back when you are out this way again. The flowers refresh on a rotation.

Odom finally cracked open the part of the ledger most pilots never see. Said I had cleared the floor. Talked about putting credits to work in places that pay back over time. There are stations in the expanse that run structured returns on parked capital. Terms on the card, he said. I am now one of the people who knows the card exists. He named one person at the end: Ilse Vey at Meridian. Did not elaborate. Will remember the name.

First session at the Verdant council. Three delegates and a mayor, three different ideas of what the bill was about. The vote went the way it went. I came out of it with a clearer picture of who carries which piece of the room. Hask anchors the soil. Drift carries the data. Marsh holds the water number. Vance keeps the floor moving. I need to know which argument moved which delegate before I come back.

Got the concession out before the vote. The bill failed on the floor. The delegate I worked in mediation came out of the session with a position that shifted two steps. Off the record, but it was real. The vote was not the only thing on the table tonight. Worth something next time the same room reopens.

Had the count before the session opened. Two pre-commits in the corridor, one of them quiet. The vote was the easy part. The work was in the conversations the delegates do not name on the record. The bill passed. The corridor work paid for the floor.

First session at the Alliance Congress in Haven's Rest. Four delegates and four different reads on the same resolution. Wentworth tracks precedent. Shirane drafts the deal. Vasc holds the principle. Tejada speaks for the Reach. The room runs slower than Verdant and the stakes scale across settlements. I need to know which delegate moves on which lever before I come back next year.

Had a delegate locked before the session and watched the pre-commit break mid-arc. The room saw it. The vote went sideways for a round. I rebuilt what I could and walked out with something. Not the win I came for. Better than nothing. Note for the next cycle: a pre-commit is good for as long as the conditions that bought it hold.

Carried a Congress vote that ripples beyond Haven's Rest. Markets at two settlements moved on the resolution. The work was building a coalition that held in two settlements at once, not one. The Alliance feels different from this side of a Congress win. Bigger room. Slower clock. The vote stays.

First session at the Guild arbitration floor. Malia runs it like a transaction: no drama, just position and record. The delegates came in knowing what they wanted. Two of them had been in the same room before. The vote resolved. I walked out with a cleaner picture of what the Guild holds and what it does not. The Reach runs on informal consensus. The arbitration floor is where the informal gets written down.

Got the concession before the vote closed. The floor did not hold, but the work in the corridor moved one delegate enough to take it off the record. Malia wrote it neutral. The Guild field rights stay unchanged for now. What shifted was the delegate's position on the next case. I know what the concession cost and what it bought. Both were real.

Ran the mediation myself. Malia stepped back and recorded. The floor was cleaner than I expected. The position I had to hold was not the one I argued for, which meant two delegates heard something they trusted more than a pitch. The session closed on a win. Malia said nothing at the end. That seems settled.
First session. Lot prices here move differently than the commodity floor. The ceiling is whatever the room will pay. Sable said two words the whole time. Both were correct.
Won my first lot. Took it at the price the room held. Filed the receipt. Next time I will know what that price meant.
A name showed up on the floor. They bid on the lot I wanted. Lost that one. I know what the lot was. I know who took it.
